  --color-text-soft: #404654;
  --color-brand: #2b59ff;
}
/* DARK — the SAME tokens, new values                          */
[data-theme="dark"] {
  --color-bg: #11151c;
  --color-surface: #1b212c;
  --color-text: #e8ecf3;
  --color-text-soft: #9aa3b2;
  --color-brand: #5b82ff;
}
* { box-sizing: border-box; }
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; background: var(--color-bg); padding: 32px; }
/* every colour below is a ROLE — no raw hex, no theme awareness */
.card {
  background: var(--color-surface);
  border-radius: 10px; padding: 24px; max-width: 420px;
}
.card h2 { color: var(--color-text); margin-top: 0; }
.card p { color: var(--color-text-soft); line-height: 1.6; }
.btn {
  background: var(--color-brand); color: #fff;
  border: none; border-radius: 6px; padding: 10px 16px;
  font-weight: 700; cursor: pointer; margin-top: 12px;
}

Now switch themes:

  1. Click "Toggle theme." The entire card flips to dark — background, text, button — and not one CSS rule for .card changed. Every component asked for a role; only the role's value changed.
  2. Hard-code .card's background to #ffffff instead of the token. Toggle to dark — the card stays glaringly white. That bright patch is what a raw colour does to a themeable UI. Fix it back.
  3. Open DevTools, select the <html> element, and change data-theme by hand between light and dark — the same instant re-theme, from the token layer alone.
Expected result: a card that switches cleanly between a light and a dark theme on a button click. The component CSS never mentions a theme — it speaks only in roles, and the theme is one swappable block of token values.
Module 4 · Chapter 4.4

Architecting Tokens at Scale

A flat list of tokens works for a small product. A large one needs the tokens themselves to be architected — organised into tiers, each referencing the one below. This three-tier structure is how design systems stay coherent across dozens of products and hundreds of components.

Lesson 4.4The Token Architecture

Concept Overview

A mature token system has three tiers, each referencing the tier below. Tier 1 — primitive tokens (also called option or core tokens): the raw palette of every value the system owns, named by what they are — --blue-500, --grey-900, --size-4. Tier 2 — semantic tokens: named by role, referencing primitives — --color-action: var(--blue-500), --color-surface: var(--grey-50). Tier 3 — component tokens: named by component, referencing semantic tokens — --button-bg: var(--color-action). Components consume only Tier 3 (or Tier 2). A value thus flows down a chain — primitive → semantic → component — and any tier can be re-pointed without disturbing the tiers above it. This is the architecture that makes a token system survive real scale.

Plain English Explanation

Think of a company's org chart. At the bottom are individual people (the primitives — concrete, specific). In the middle are roles like "the finance lead" (the semantic tier — a role that some specific person currently fills). At the top, a project says "we need the finance lead's sign-off" (the component tier — it refers to the role, not the person). When the finance lead changes, the projects do not need rewriting — they referred to the role, and the role now points to a different person.

Token tiers work the same way. Tier 1 is the concrete palette: --blue-500 is exactly #2b59ff, a specific thing. Tier 2 is the roles: --color-action means "the colour of primary actions," and it currently points at --blue-500. Tier 3 is what each component asks for: --button-bg points at --color-action. Now you can make decisions at the right level. Rebrand the blue? Change Tier 1 — every tier above follows. Decide actions should be green, not blue? Re-point Tier 2 — components follow. Want just buttons a different shade while other actions stay? Change Tier 3 — nothing else moves. Each kind of change has exactly one place it belongs, and that is what keeps a huge system sane.

Why It Matters

A flat token list cannot express the difference between "change the brand blue" and "change only the button colour" — both are just edits to flat tokens, and it is easy to make one when you meant the other, affecting far more or far less than intended. Tiers give each kind of change a correct, single home: brand-wide changes belong in Tier 1, role changes in Tier 2, component-specific tweaks in Tier 3. This is what lets a large organisation let many people change the system safely — the architecture itself constrains the blast radius of each change. Without tiers, a token system at scale slowly becomes as tangled as the raw values it replaced.

Real-World Example

A product has primary buttons, link colours, and focus rings — all currently the brand blue. Two change requests arrive. First: "the brand is moving from blue to indigo." With tiers, that is one edit to the Tier 1 primitive --blue-500 — buttons, links, and focus rings all follow, because all three trace through --color-action down to that primitive. Second, later: "make the focus ring a distinct high-visibility colour, but leave buttons and links on the brand colour." With tiers, that is one edit to a single Tier 3 component token, --focus-ring-color — buttons and links are untouched because they reference a different Tier 3 token. Two very different-scoped changes, each a precise one-line edit, each affecting exactly what was intended. A flat list could not have kept those two requests from interfering.

Enterprise Example

At full enterprise scale the three-tier architecture is the design system's backbone, and it is what makes multi-brand and multi-product possible. The Tier 1 primitive palette and the Tier 3 component tokens stay largely fixed; a brand is essentially a distinct Tier 2 semantic mapping — the same component tokens, the same primitive palette, a different wiring of roles between them. A theme (light/dark/high-contrast) is also a Tier 2 variation. The whole system is typically defined in a tool-agnostic format (the emerging standard is the W3C Design Tokens format — JSON) and compiled by a build step into CSS custom properties, native mobile resources, and the design tool's variables, so every platform consumes one source of truth. This is why a large company's web app, iOS app, and marketing site can feel like one product: they are all the same token graph, compiled for different targets. The three tiers are what keep that graph navigable as it grows to thousands of tokens.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

How to architect a three-tier token system:

  1. Build Tier 1 — the primitive palette. Every raw colour, every spacing-scale and type-scale value, named by what it is: --blue-500, --space-4. No component touches this tier directly.
  2. Build Tier 2 — the semantic layer. A role token for every meaningful use: --color-surface, --color-action, --color-danger, --text-body. Each is var() of a Tier 1 primitive.
  3. Build Tier 3 — component tokens, where useful. For components needing their own knob: --button-bg: var(--color-action). Simple components may reference Tier 2 directly and skip Tier 3.
  4. Wire components to the top tier only. A component reads Tier 3, or Tier 2 — never a Tier 1 primitive.
  5. Make changes at the correct tier: palette-wide in Tier 1, role-wide in Tier 2, component-specific in Tier 3. Themes and brands are Tier 2 variations.
  6. Keep the source tool-agnostic if the system spans platforms — define tokens once (e.g. as JSON) and compile to CSS, native, and design-tool formats.

Code Example

css
CSS — the three token tiers:root {
  /* TIER 1 — PRIMITIVES: raw palette, named by what they are. */
  --blue-500:  #2b59ff;
  --red-500:   #c2304a;
  --grey-50:   #ffffff;
  --grey-900:  #16267a;

  /* TIER 2 — SEMANTIC: named by role, point at primitives.    */
  --color-action:      var(--blue-500);
  --color-destructive: var(--red-500);
  --color-surface:     var(--grey-50);
  --color-text:        var(--grey-900);

  /* TIER 3 — COMPONENT: named by component, point at semantic.*/
  --button-bg:        var(--color-action);
  --button-danger-bg: var(--color-destructive);
}

/* The component reads ONLY the top tier.                      */
.button          { background: var(--button-bg); }
.button.danger   { background: var(--button-danger-bg); }

Responsive Example

The tier architecture composes with everything earlier in this module. The Tier 1 primitives include the fluid clamp() scale steps from Chapter 4.2; the Tier 2 semantic layer includes the light/dark theme variations from Chapter 4.3. A single component token at Tier 3 therefore inherits fluid and themeable behaviour for free:

css
CSS — tiers carrying fluid and themeable behaviour:root {
  /* TIER 1: a fluid primitive (Chapter 2.3 + 4.2)             */
  --size-pad-lg: clamp(16px, 3vw, 40px);
  /* TIER 2: a semantic role pointing at it                    */
  --space-section: var(--size-pad-lg);
  /* TIER 3: a component token pointing at the role            */
  --panel-padding: var(--space-section);
}
.panel { padding: var(--panel-padding); } /* fluid, with zero awareness */

The .panel rule is one line and knows nothing of fluidity or themes — yet it scales smoothly and themes correctly, because those behaviours live in the tiers below it. That is the architecture paying off: behaviour is layered in once, low down, and inherited by everything above.

Common Mistakes

Common Mistake

Components referencing Tier 1 directly. A component using var(--blue-500) instead of a semantic or component token has skipped the architecture — it cannot be re-themed, and a brand change at Tier 1 will move it unexpectedly. Components touch the top tier only.

Common Mistake

Over-building Tier 3. Not every component needs its own token tier. A component token is justified when the component genuinely needs an independent knob; otherwise referencing Tier 2 directly is simpler. Tiers are a tool, not a quota.

Common Mistake

Semantic tokens that are really primitives. A "semantic" token named --color-blue has not done its job — it still names a value, not a role. The Tier 2 layer must be genuinely role-based (--color-action) or it provides no decoupling.

Principal Engineer Notes

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer, before making a token change, asks "what is the scope of this change?" — and that question selects the tier. Brand-wide means Tier 1; a role's meaning means Tier 2; one component means Tier 3. Choosing the right tier is choosing the correct blast radius, and a change made at the wrong tier either breaks more than intended or has to be repeated. The tier is the answer to "where does this belong," and they always ask the question first.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

They keep the system tool-agnostic at its source. The tokens are defined once, in a neutral format, and compiled to CSS, to native platforms, and to design tools — rather than hand-maintained separately in each. They know that the moment the same token exists in two hand-edited places, the two will drift, and the single-source-of-truth guarantee — the entire point of tokens — is lost. One source, many compiled targets.

Exercises

Practice — Token Architecture
  1. Build all three tiers for a small button system: a primitive palette, a semantic layer, and component tokens. Wire two button variants to Tier 3 only.
  2. Make a "brand change": edit one Tier 1 primitive and confirm every component that traces to it updates.
  3. Make a "role change": re-point a Tier 2 semantic token to a different primitive and confirm the right components follow.
  4. Make a "component-only change": edit one Tier 3 token and confirm only that component changes while everything sharing the same role stays put. You have now exercised all three blast radii.
Solution & What to Check
  1. Tier 1 is the raw palette (--blue-500); Tier 2 the semantic roles (--color-action: var(--blue-500)); Tier 3 the component tokens (--button-bg: var(--color-action)). Components reference Tier 3 only.
  2. A Tier 1 edit is the widest blast radius — everything tracing to that primitive follows.
  3. A Tier 2 edit is medium — everything playing that role follows, but other roles on the same primitive do not.
  4. A Tier 3 edit is narrowest — only that one component changes. Each kind of change has exactly one correct tier; choosing the tier is choosing the blast radius.

Architecture Thinking

The three-tier token system is layered architecture in miniature, and it teaches the principle in a form small enough to hold whole. Each layer depends only on the layer directly below, names things in its own appropriate vocabulary (values, then roles, then components), and exposes a stable surface to the layer above. A change is made at the layer that owns that kind of decision, and its effect propagates predictably upward. This is the same shape as a well-layered application — data layer, domain layer, presentation layer — and the same discipline pays off the same way: each layer can be reasoned about and changed in isolation, and the system scales because complexity is partitioned rather than tangled. If you understand why token tiers work, you understand why layered architecture works; it is one idea at two scales.

Debugging Tips

  • Trace the chain in the Computed tab. DevTools resolves a var() to its final value; follow a component token to its semantic token to its primitive to confirm the wiring is correct.
  • A change that did too much or too little was made at the wrong tier. If a button tweak also moved links, it was made in Tier 2 when it belonged in Tier 3 — and vice versa.
  • Grep for primitive names in component CSS. A component file mentioning --blue-500 directly is a tier violation; it should reference a semantic or component token instead.

Performance Considerations

Performance

A three-tier chain means a var() may resolve through two or three references before reaching a literal — and this is genuinely free; the style engine resolves the chain once during style computation, and the depth is trivial. There is no runtime penalty for well-architected tiers. The performance benefit is again indirect and real: a tiered system has a single source of truth compiled to one stylesheet, which keeps CSS small and free of duplicated values, and a smaller stylesheet is parsed and applied faster. The one thing to keep in mind is the same as Chapter 4.1: changing a token that sits near the bottom of the tree and is read by thousands of elements triggers a wide style recalculation — fine for a theme switch, but not something to animate every frame. Module 7 covers style recalculation cost.

Build This — Mini-Project
A Three-Tier Token System

Build the full token architecture — primitive, semantic, component — and wire two button variants to the top tier only. Then make three changes, one at each tier, and watch each one affect exactly the scope it should: the whole palette, a role, or a single component.

Goal: two buttons whose colours resolve through all three token tiers, so each kind of change has one correct home.
html
index.html<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Token Tiers</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
</head>
<body>
  <button class="btn btn-primary">Primary</button>
  <button class="btn btn-danger">Delete</button>
</body>
</html>
css
styles.css:root {
  /* TIER 1 — PRIMITIVES: raw values, named by what they are.  */
  --blue-500: #2b59ff;
  --red-500:  #c2304a;
  --white:    #ffffff;

  /* TIER 2 — SEMANTIC: named by role, reference primitives.   */
  --color-action:      var(--blue-500);
  --color-destructive: var(--red-500);
  --color-on-action:   var(--white);

  /* TIER 3 — COMPONENT: named by component, reference semantic.*/
  --btn-primary-bg: var(--color-action);
  --btn-danger-bg:  var(--color-destructive);
  --btn-fg:         var(--color-on-action);
}
* { box-sizing: border-box; }
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; background: #eef1f5; padding: 32px; }
/* the component reads ONLY tier 3                             */
.btn {
  border: none; border-radius: 8px; padding: 12px 20px;
  font-weight: 700; color: var(--btn-fg); cursor: pointer; margin-right: 8px;
}
.btn-primary { background: var(--btn-primary-bg); }
.btn-danger  { background: var(--btn-danger-bg); }

Now make a change at each tier and watch the blast radius:

  1. Tier 1 (brand-wide): change --blue-500 to #6d4aff. The primary button turns purple — and so would links, focus rings, anything tracing to that primitive. Widest scope.
  2. Tier 2 (role-wide): re-point --color-action to var(--red-500). Everything playing the "action" role follows. Medium scope.
  3. Tier 3 (component-only): change --btn-primary-bg to #0f8a4d directly. Only the primary button moves — the danger button and every other action are untouched. Narrowest scope.
  4. In DevTools' Computed tab, click the primary button and trace background down the chain: component → semantic → primitive → literal.
Expected result: two buttons whose colours resolve through three token tiers. A Tier 1 edit re-themes broadly, a Tier 2 edit re-themes a role, a Tier 3 edit changes one component — each kind of change with exactly one correct home.
05
Module Five

Component Architecture

Modern interfaces are not pages — they are components: self-contained, reusable pieces composed into screens. This module is about the discipline that keeps those components isolated, predictable, and reusable: what each component owns, where its CSS lives, and how primitives compose into a real UI.

5.1Component-First Thinking
5.2Global CSS vs Local CSS — When to Use Each
5.3Building Reusable Primitives & Dashboard Components
Module 5 · Chapter 5.1

Component-First Thinking

A page is a thing you build once. A component is a thing you build once and use everywhere. Shifting from thinking in pages to thinking in components is the single biggest change in how professional frontends are built — and it rests on one rule about what a component is allowed to own.

Lesson 5.1Building in Components

Concept Overview

A component is a self-contained, reusable piece of interface — a button, a card, a modal — that bundles its structure (HTML) and its appearance (CSS) into one unit that can be dropped anywhere. The defining rule of component architecture is the ownership boundary: a component owns its inside — its internal layout, padding, borders, colours — and owns nothing outside itself. It has no outer margin, makes no assumption about where it sits, and does not reach out to style its neighbours. External spacing — the gap between components — belongs to the layout that places them. "Components own their inside; layouts own the gaps" is the rule that makes a component genuinely reusable, because a component that assumes nothing about its surroundings can be placed in any surroundings.

Plain English Explanation

Think of a component like a piece of well-designed furniture — a chair. A good chair is complete in itself: it has its own structure, its own finish, and it works the same whether you put it in a kitchen, an office, or a waiting room. Crucially, the chair does not come with a fixed amount of floor space glued to its legs. How far it sits from the next chair is decided by whoever arranges the room, not by the chair. A chair that demanded "always 50cm of empty space on my left" would be nearly impossible to arrange.

A UI component works the same way. A card should be complete in itself — its padding, its border, its rounded corners, its internal layout all built in — and it should work identically whether it is dropped into a dashboard grid, a sidebar, or a modal. But it must not carry its own outer spacing. If a card has margin-bottom: 24px baked in, then every place that uses the card inherits that 24px whether it wants it or not, and arranging cards becomes a fight. Instead, the card owns only its inside, and the layout — the grid or stack that places the cards — owns the gap between them. The component is the chair; the layout is the room arranger. Keep that line clean and components become things you can truly reuse.

Why It Matters

The component is the unit of reuse, and reuse is what makes a large frontend sustainable. A button built once and used in two hundred places is two hundred places that are automatically consistent and that all improve when the button improves. But reuse only works if the component is genuinely context-independent — and the ownership boundary is what guarantees that. A component with a baked-in outer margin, or one that styles its parent, is secretly coupled to one specific context; move it and it breaks. Component-first thinking, with a strict ownership boundary, is what lets a team build a screen by composing existing, trusted pieces instead of writing every screen from scratch.

Real-World Example

A team builds a StatCard for their dashboard and, because the dashboard needs 24px between cards, bakes margin-bottom: 24px into the card itself. It works — on the dashboard. Then the card is reused inside a modal, where it is the only element, and now there is a mysterious 24px of dead space below it. It is reused in a tight sidebar, where the 24px is too much. It is reused in a horizontal row, where margin-bottom does nothing useful at all. Each context needs an override fighting the baked-in margin. The component-first fix: the StatCard has no outer margin, and the dashboard places its cards in a grid with gap: 24px. Now the exact same card drops cleanly into the modal, the sidebar, and the row — each layout supplies its own spacing, and the card never has to be fought.

Enterprise Example

At enterprise scale, components are the deliverable of a design system: a shared library of buttons, inputs, cards, modals, and tables that every product team consumes. The ownership boundary is what makes that library trustworthy. Because each component owns only its inside, the design-system team can guarantee its behaviour without knowing every context it will be dropped into — and product teams can compose screens from the library without reading its source. The same discipline maps directly onto component frameworks like React, Svelte, or Vue (the stack this course's examples generalise to): a framework component bundles markup and scoped styles into exactly the self-contained unit described here, and the "own your inside, not your outside" rule is what keeps a framework component reusable. Enterprise frontend velocity — shipping screens fast — comes almost entirely from having a library of well-bounded components to compose. Module 8's projects are built this way.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

How to build a component with a clean ownership boundary:

  1. Define the component's structure as a self-contained piece of HTML with a single root element and a clear block name.
  2. Style the component's inside: its internal layout, padding, border, background, colours — drawing values from tokens (Module 4).
  3. Give the component no outer margin and no assumption about its width beyond what it genuinely needs — let the layout size and space it.
  4. Never let the component style elements outside itself, and never let it target its parent or siblings.
  5. Place the component using a layout (a Grid or a Stack primitive from Module 3); the layout owns the gap between component instances.
  6. Test reuse: drop the same component into two different layouts. If it needs no per-context override, the boundary is clean.

Code Example

css
CSS — a StatCard component, and the layout that places it/* THE COMPONENT — owns only its inside. No outer margin.      */
.stat-card {
  background: var(--color-surface);
  border-radius: var(--radius);
  padding: var(--space-4);
  /* NO margin here — spacing is not the component's job.      */
}
.stat-card__label { color: var(--color-text-soft); }
.stat-card__value { font-size: var(--text-xl); font-weight: 700; }

/* THE LAYOUT — owns the gaps BETWEEN component instances.     */
.dashboard-grid {
  display: grid;
  gap: var(--space-4);  /* the spacing lives HERE, not in the card */
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr));
}

Responsive Example

The ownership boundary is what makes a component responsive across every context it lands in. Because the component owns its inside and the layout owns placement, the same card is correct in a multi-column dashboard grid, a single-column mobile stack, and a sidebar — each layout reflows independently, and the card simply fills whatever space it is given:

css
CSS — one component, three responsive layouts, no overrides/* The component is written ONCE — context-independent.        */
.stat-card { background: var(--color-surface); padding: var(--space-4); }

/* Each layout places it differently — the card is untouched.  */
.dashboard-grid { display: grid; gap: 16px;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr)); }
.sidebar-stack { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 8px; }

This is the reuse payoff made concrete: the responsive behaviour of a screen lives in its layouts (Module 3's primitives), and the component is a context-free building block that those layouts arrange. Write the component once; let many responsive layouts place it.

Common Mistakes

Common Mistake

An outer margin baked into a component. The single most common boundary violation. It makes the component carry spacing that belongs to the layout, and every reuse context inherits spacing it did not ask for. Components have no outer margin.

Common Mistake

A component styling its surroundings. A component whose CSS targets its parent, its siblings, or the page reaches outside its boundary and becomes coupled to one specific context. A component styles only itself and its own descendants.

Common Mistake

Context baked into the component. A card that hard-codes width: 320px because "that is how wide it is on the dashboard" can no longer fill a narrow sidebar or a wide modal. Let the layout size the component; the component should be flexible about its own width.

Principal Engineer Notes

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer, building any component, asks "could this drop into a context I have not thought of yet?" — and the honest answer requires a clean boundary. They treat an outer margin on a component as a defect in review, every time, because they have seen exactly how it breaks on the third or fourth reuse. The discipline is not pedantry; it is the accumulated scar tissue of reusing components that were not built to be reused.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

They keep a sharp mental separation between layout components and content components. A layout component (a Grid, a Stack — Module 3's primitives) arranges things and owns gaps. A content component (a card, a form, a chart) presents something and owns its inside. The two never blur: a card does not arrange, a grid does not present. Keeping those two jobs in two different kinds of component is what makes a UI tree clean and composable.

Exercises

Practice — Component-First Thinking
  1. Build a StatCard component with a clean boundary — internal padding and styling, no outer margin. Render several in a grid whose gap supplies the spacing.
  2. Now bake margin-bottom: 24px into the card. Drop one into a modal and observe the stray dead space. Drop one into a horizontal row and observe the margin do nothing useful. Remove it.
  3. Take the clean card and place it in three different layouts — a grid, a vertical stack, a sidebar — with no per-context override. Confirm it works in all three.
  4. Find a component that hard-codes its width. Make it flexible and confirm it now fills both a narrow and a wide container correctly.
Solution & What to Check
  1. A clean StatCard has internal padding and styling but zero outer margin — the DevTools box model should show padding and border only. The grid's gap supplies the spacing.
  2. The baked-in margin-bottom: 24px shows as stray dead space in a modal and does nothing useful in a horizontal row — proof that outer spacing belongs to the layout, not the component.
  3. The clean card drops into a grid, a stack, and a sidebar with no per-context override — that is what a clean ownership boundary buys.
  4. A hard-coded width stops the component filling a narrow or wide container; making the width flexible lets the layout size it correctly in both.

Architecture Thinking

The ownership boundary is encapsulation — the most fundamental idea in software architecture — expressed in CSS. An encapsulated unit has a clear inside that it controls and a clear outside that it does not touch, and it communicates with the rest of the system only through a deliberate, narrow interface. A well-bounded component is exactly that: its inside is its own, its outside is the layout's, and its "interface" is the slot it occupies. This is why component architecture scales — a system of encapsulated parts can grow large without growing tangled, because each part can be understood, tested, and changed in isolation. Module 3's layout primitives were encapsulated layout; Module 4's tokens were encapsulated values; this chapter is encapsulated UI. The whole course has been teaching one principle — bound things cleanly so they compose — at every layer of the frontend.

Debugging Tips

  • Inspect a component for outer margin. Select the component's root element; if the DevTools box model shows any margin, the boundary is leaking. It should show padding and border only.
  • Drop the component somewhere unexpected. The fastest test of a boundary is to place the component in a context it was not built for. Anything that breaks reveals a hidden assumption.
  • Check whether the component's CSS targets anything outside itself. Search its rules for selectors that reach a parent or sibling — those are boundary violations and a source of action-at-a-distance bugs.

Performance Considerations

Performance

Component architecture itself has no rendering cost — a component is just HTML and CSS — but it has a real performance benefit at the system level. When UI is built from a bounded set of reused components, the same CSS rules apply to many elements, so the stylesheet stays small and the browser's style engine has fewer distinct rules to evaluate. A page-based codebase, by contrast, accumulates near-duplicate CSS per page, bloating the stylesheet that every user must download and that the engine must process. In framework terms, scoped component styles also let build tools ship only the CSS for the components actually on the page (code-splitting). Reuse keeps CSS small; small CSS is fast. Module 7 covers stylesheet size and style recalculation directly.

Build This — Mini-Project
A Reusable StatCard with a Clean Boundary

Build a StatCard component the right way — it owns its inside and has no outer margin — then place several in a grid whose gap supplies the spacing. You will see the same component used twice, identical and context-free, and prove the boundary by checking it carries no outer margin.

Goal: a reusable stat card with internal styling only, placed by a layout that owns the gaps between instances.
html
index.html<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Component-First</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
</head>
<body>
  <!-- the LAYOUT places the components and owns the gaps -->
  <div class="layout">
    <!-- two instances of the SAME component -->
    <article class="stat-card">
      <p class="stat-card__label">Monthly Revenue</p>
      <p class="stat-card__value">$48,250</p>
      <p class="stat-card__delta stat-card__delta--up">+12.4%</p>
    </article>
    <article class="stat-card">
      <p class="stat-card__label">Active Users</p>
      <p class="stat-card__value">8,910</p>
      <p class="stat-card__delta stat-card__delta--down">-3.1%</p>
    </article>
  </div>
</body>
</html>
css
styles.css* { box-sizing: border-box; }
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; background: #eef1f5; padding: 32px; }

/* THE LAYOUT — owns the gap BETWEEN components.               */
.layout { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 16px; }

/* THE COMPONENT — owns only its inside. NO outer margin.      */
.stat-card {
  background: #fff;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px;
  flex: 1 1 220px; /* flexible width — the layout decides */
}
.stat-card__label { margin: 0; color: #6b7280; font-size: 13px; }
.stat-card__value { margin: 4px 0; color: #16267a; font-size: 28px; font-weight: 700; }
.stat-card__delta { margin: 0; font-size: 13px; font-weight: 700; }
.stat-card__delta--up   { color: #0f8a4d; }
.stat-card__delta--down { color: #c2304a; }

Now test the boundary:

  1. Both cards render identically — one component definition, two instances. The gap between them comes from .layout, not the card.
  2. Inspect a .stat-card in DevTools. The box model shows padding and border but zero margin — the boundary is clean.
  3. Now add margin-bottom: 24px to .stat-card. The cards still look fine here — but that baked-in margin is exactly what would break the card the day it is reused in a modal. Remove it again.
Expected result: two identical stat cards in a row, evenly spaced by the layout's gap. The card carries no outer margin — proof of a clean ownership boundary, and the reason this exact card could drop into any layout unchanged.
Module 5 · Chapter 5.2

Global CSS vs Local CSS — When to Use Each

CSS is global by default — every rule can affect every element on the page. That default is the source of most large-codebase CSS pain. The discipline that fixes it is knowing exactly what belongs in the small global layer and what must be scoped locally to a component.

Lesson 5.2Scoping Styles Deliberately

Concept Overview

By default, a CSS rule has global scope: write button { ... } and it styles every button on every page. That is powerful and dangerous. The professional approach splits CSS into two deliberate layers. The global layer is small and stable: the reset, the design tokens, base typography, and a few truly universal rules — the things that genuinely should apply everywhere. The local layer is everything else: each component's styles, scoped so they affect only that component and cannot leak out or be leaked into. Scoping is achieved by convention — a naming methodology such as BEM (Block, Element, Modifier) that gives every component a unique class namespace — or by tooling, such as a framework's scoped or module styles. The rule of thumb: global for what is universal, local for everything that belongs to one component.

Plain English Explanation

Imagine an office where, by default, anything you say is broadcast on a building-wide loudspeaker. A few announcements genuinely should go to everyone — "the fire exits are here," "lunch is at noon." But most conversations should stay in one room. If every conversation goes out on the loudspeaker, the building becomes chaos: everyone hears everything, and no one can tell which message was meant for them.

CSS is that loudspeaker office. A bare rule like h2 { color: blue } is broadcast building-wide — it hits every <h2> in every component. A little of that is right: the reset, the tokens, the base font genuinely are building-wide announcements. But a component's own styles are a private conversation — the card's title colour is the card's business, and it should not be shouted at every other component's titles. Scoping is how you keep a conversation in its room. The simplest way is a naming convention: instead of styling h2, you style .card__title — a class name unique to the card. Now the rule can only ever hit elements that opted in by carrying that exact class. The card's styles stay in the card's room; another component's .modal__title is a separate conversation entirely. Keep the loudspeaker for the few genuine announcements; keep everything else local.

Why It Matters

Unscoped global CSS is the primary reason large CSS codebases become frightening to change. When any rule can affect any element, no change is safely local: edit a global button rule and you might have altered a button three teams away that you have never seen. Engineers stop deleting old CSS — they cannot prove it is unused — so the stylesheet only ever grows. They stop editing existing rules — too risky — and instead add new, more-specific rules to override the old ones, and specificity spirals (the problem Chapter 1.3 warned about). A disciplined global/local split breaks this: because component styles are scoped, a change to a component is provably contained to that component. The stylesheet becomes safe to change, and therefore safe to keep clean.

Real-World Example

A team has a global rule ul { list-style: none; padding-left: 0 } — added early because the navigation menu needed it. Months later someone adds a help article with ordinary bulleted lists, and the bullets are mysteriously gone. The global rule, written for the nav, silently reached the article. The engineer "fixes" it by adding .article ul { list-style: disc } to override — and now there are two global list rules fighting, and the next list somewhere will inherit whichever wins. The local-CSS fix: there is no global ul rule at all. The navigation's list styling is scoped — .nav__list { list-style: none } — so it only ever affects the nav's list. The article's bullets were never touched, no override was needed, and no fight was started. One unscoped global rule created a bug and an override; the scoped version simply has neither.

Enterprise Example

At enterprise scale, with dozens of engineers in one codebase, unscoped global CSS is unworkable — there is no way for one engineer to know every rule that might be affected by a change. So enterprises enforce scoping, by tooling rather than willpower. Component frameworks provide it directly: a framework's scoped styles or CSS Modules automatically rewrite a component's class names to be unique, so a component's CSS is guaranteed to affect only that component, with no naming discipline required. Utility-class approaches reach the same goal differently — a fixed vocabulary of tiny global classes that compose, so almost no bespoke CSS is written at all. Either way, the global layer is deliberately tiny — reset, tokens, base — and everything else is mechanically scoped. The native platform is catching up too: real CSS scoping (the @scope rule) and the Shadow DOM provide browser-level encapsulation. The enterprise principle is constant regardless of the tool: global is a small, audited, deliberate layer; everything else is scoped.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

How to organise CSS into a global and a local layer:

  1. Decide the global layer's contents — and keep the list short: the reset (e.g. box-sizing), the design tokens on :root, base element defaults (body font, default text colour), and nothing else.
  2. For every component, define a block name — a unique class that namespaces it: .card, .modal, .toolbar.
  3. Scope every component rule under its block name. With BEM, the block's parts are .block__element and variants are .block--modifier or .block__element--modifier.
  4. Never write a bare element selector (h2, ul, button) in the local layer — always go through a scoped class.
  5. Where the toolchain allows, let it enforce scoping automatically — framework scoped styles or CSS Modules — so correctness does not depend on discipline.
  6. Audit the global layer periodically. If something universal-looking has crept in that really belongs to one component, move it into that component's local scope.

Code Example

css
CSS — a small global layer and scoped local components/* ===== GLOBAL LAYER — small, stable, deliberately universal. */
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; }          /* reset   */
:root { --brand: #2b59ff; --surface: #fff; }  /* tokens  */
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; }           /* base    */

/* ===== LOCAL LAYER — every rule scoped under a block name.   */
/* BEM: .block  .block__element  .block__element--modifier     */
.card { background: var(--surface); padding: 20px; }
.card__title { color: #16267a; }
.card__action { background: var(--brand); }

/* a DIFFERENT component — its own scope. No collision with    */
/* .card__action even though both are "action" buttons.        */
.sidebar__action { background: #0f8a4d; }

/* AVOID in the local layer — a bare selector is global:       */
/*   button { background: ... }  hits EVERY button everywhere. */

Responsive Example

The global/local split applies to responsive CSS too. A truly global responsive concern — a fluid token, a base font that scales — belongs in the global layer. But a component's own responsive behaviour belongs scoped to that component, and modern CSS lets it respond to the component's own container rather than the viewport, using the container queries from Module 3:

css
CSS — a component's responsive rules, scoped to itself/* the component is its own container-query context           */
.media-card { container-type: inline-size; }

/* this responsive rule is SCOPED — it restyles only the       */
/* media-card, based on the media-card's own width.            */
@container (min-width: 340px) {
  .media-card__layout { display: flex; gap: 16px; }
}

Scoped, container-driven responsive rules are the most local form of responsiveness possible: the component's adaptive behaviour belongs entirely to the component, depends only on the component's own size, and cannot interfere with anything else. Global responsiveness for global things; local responsiveness for components.

Native CSS Nesting

For years, scoping a component's styles under its block name required either repeating the block name on every rule (.card, .card__title, .card__action) or running the CSS through a preprocessor like Sass to nest the rules. Modern CSS has native nesting built in, baseline across every major browser since 2023. A component's local rules can now be authored as a single nested block with no preprocessor — the syntax browsers run directly.

Inside a rule, an indented child rule applies to descendants that match its selector. The & character refers to the parent selector explicitly, which is needed for element-and-modifier patterns like &.is-open.

css
CSS — the same component, written two ways/* BEFORE — flat with the block name repeated each time:        */
.card { background: var(--color-surface); padding: var(--space-3); }
.card__title { color: var(--color-text); }
.card__action { background: var(--color-brand); }
.card.is-open { box-shadow: var(--shadow-lg); }

/* AFTER — native nesting; the block name appears ONCE.         */
.card {
  background: var(--color-surface);
  padding: var(--space-3);

  .card__title  { color: var(--color-text); }
  .card__action { background: var(--color-brand); }

  /* & refers to the parent selector — for modifier patterns:    */
  &.is-open { box-shadow: var(--shadow-lg); }
}

Two cautions about nesting. First, deep nesting raises specificity — nesting .card .card__title matches with the combined specificity of both, which can start the exact spirals Chapter 1.3 warned about. Keep nesting one level deep wherever possible; treat each nested rule as still flat per element. Second, nesting is organisational, not scoping — a rule nested inside .card still matches any element with that class anywhere on the page. Real scoping still comes from BEM-style class naming or framework scoped styles; nesting just lets you author the scoped CSS more compactly.

Common Mistakes

Common Mistake

Bare element selectors in component CSS. Writing h2 { ... } or ul { ... } inside what should be a component's styles silently makes that rule global. Component rules go through a scoped class, always.

Common Mistake

A global layer that keeps growing. The global layer should be small and stable. If component-specific rules keep being added to it "because it was quick," it becomes the unscoped chaos the split was meant to prevent. Guard the global layer's contents.

Common Mistake

Deep descendant selectors as fake scoping. .card div span a { ... } is not scoping — it is a fragile, highly specific selector that breaks when the markup changes. Real scoping is a flat, single, well-named class per element.

Principal Engineer Notes

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer treats the global layer as a guarded, almost sacred space. Adding something to it is a deliberate decision that must be justified — "is this genuinely universal?" — because every global rule is a rule that can reach anywhere. Their default answer to "where does this CSS go?" is "scoped to a component," and global is the rare, argued-for exception.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

They value scoping that is enforced by tooling over scoping that depends on discipline. A naming convention like BEM works only as long as every engineer follows it perfectly forever; framework scoped styles or CSS Modules make scope-correctness automatic and impossible to get wrong. A principal engineer reaches for the mechanism that removes the human error, because at scale, human error is a certainty.

Exercises

Practice — Global vs Local
  1. Build a stylesheet with a clearly separated global layer (reset, tokens, base) and a local layer of two BEM-scoped components.
  2. Add a global button { background: red } rule and watch it reach every button in both components. Then remove it and scope the styling to each component instead — confirm the collision is gone.
  3. Build two components that both have an "action" element. Using BEM (.card__action, .sidebar__action), confirm their styles never collide despite the shared concept.
  4. Take a deep descendant selector like .card div span and rewrite it as a single scoped class. Change the markup nesting and confirm the scoped version survives while the descendant version would not.
Solution & What to Check
  1. The global layer holds only reset, tokens, and base element defaults; everything else is scoped under a component block name. If a component-specific rule is in the global layer, move it out.
  2. The global button rule reaches every button in both components — one unscoped selector with an unknown blast radius. Scoping the styling under each component's block name removes the collision.
  3. .card__action and .sidebar__action never collide despite both being "action" buttons, because each is namespaced to its own block.
  4. A deep descendant selector breaks when the markup nesting changes; a single scoped class survives, because it matches the element directly rather than relying on a fragile ancestor path.

Architecture Thinking

The global/local split is the management of scope, and scope management is one of the oldest and most important ideas in software. A program where every variable is global is unmaintainable for exactly the same reason a stylesheet of unscoped global rules is: any change can affect anything, so nothing can be reasoned about locally. The progress of software architecture has largely been the progress of better scoping — local variables, modules, namespaces, encapsulation. CSS, because it began global-by-default, had to rediscover this, and BEM, CSS Modules, scoped styles, and @scope are all the same idea: take a global namespace and partition it into local ones so that change becomes local and therefore safe. When you keep the global layer small and scope everything else, you are applying the single principle that lets any large system stay changeable.

Debugging Tips

  • Unexpected styling? Check for a global rule. In the DevTools Styles pane, look at every rule applying to the element. A bare element selector you did not expect is a global rule reaching in.
  • Trace where a rule comes from. DevTools shows the source file and line of each rule. A component being styled by a rule from an unrelated file is a scoping leak.
  • Audit the global stylesheet directly. Open the file that holds the global layer and read it top to bottom. Anything that is not reset, tokens, or base is a candidate to move into a component's local scope.

Performance Considerations

Performance

Scoping has a genuine, if modest, performance benefit. A single, flat, well-named class selector (.card__title) is one of the fastest things the browser's style engine can match — it checks one class on one element. Deep descendant selectors (.card div span a) are slower, because the engine must walk up the ancestor chain to confirm the match for every candidate element. So scoped, flat class selectors are not only safer to change, they match faster. The larger win is again about stylesheet size: a disciplined global/local split, especially with tooling that ships only the CSS for components actually present, keeps the delivered stylesheet small — and a small stylesheet is downloaded, parsed, and applied faster. Module 7 measures selector cost and stylesheet size directly.

Build This — Mini-Project
A Small Global Layer & Two Scoped Components

Build a stylesheet split cleanly into a tiny global layer (reset, tokens, base) and a local layer of two BEM-scoped components. Both components have an "action" button — and you will prove that scoping keeps their styles from ever colliding, then watch an unscoped global rule cause exactly the collision scoping prevents.

Goal: two components with same-named parts that never collide, because each component's CSS is scoped under its own block name.
html
index.html<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Global vs Local</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
</head>
<body>
  <!-- component 1: block name "card" -->
  <div class="card">
    <h3 class="card__title">Scoped Component</h3>
    <p class="card__body">My styles are scoped by class name. Nothing leaks in.</p>
    <button class="card__action">Card Action</button>
  </div>
  <!-- component 2: block name "sidebar" -->
  <div class="sidebar">
    <button class="sidebar__action">Sidebar Action</button>
  </div>
</body>
</html>
css
styles.css/* ===== GLOBAL LAYER — small, stable, universal. =====        */
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; }
:root { --brand: #2b59ff; --surface: #ffffff; }
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; background: #eef1f5; padding: 32px; }

/* ===== LOCAL LAYER — every rule scoped under a block. =====   */
.card {
  background: var(--surface); border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px; max-width: 380px; margin-bottom: 16px;
}
.card__title { color: #16267a; font-size: 16px; }
.card__body { color: #404654; font-size: 14px; margin: 8px 0; }
/* scoped under .card -- cannot collide with .sidebar__action  */
.card__action {
  background: var(--brand); color: #fff;
  border: none; border-radius: 6px; padding: 8px 16px; font-weight: 700;
}
.sidebar {
  background: var(--surface); border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 20px; max-width: 380px;
}
/* a DIFFERENT action button -- its own scope, green, no clash  */
.sidebar__action {
  background: #0f8a4d; color: #fff;
  border: none; border-radius: 6px; padding: 8px 16px; font-weight: 700;
}

Now prove scoping works — and watch global break it:

  1. Both action buttons render in their own colour — the card's blue, the sidebar's green. They share the concept "action" but their scoped class names (card__action vs sidebar__action) never collide.
  2. Add a global rule at the top: button { background: #c2304a; }. Reload — both buttons turn red. One unscoped global rule reached into every component.
  3. Remove the global button rule. The scoped colours return. This is exactly why component styles must be scoped, never bare element selectors.
Expected result: two components, each with an "action" button in its own colour, with no collision — because each component's CSS is scoped under its block name. Adding one global button rule overrides both, demonstrating precisely the leak that scoping prevents.
Module 5 · Chapter 5.3

Building Reusable Primitives & Dashboard Components

This chapter brings the whole course together. A real dashboard component is built from layout primitives (Module 3), styled with tokens (Module 4), bounded by the ownership rule and scoped locally (this module). Composition is how a few reusable pieces become an entire interface.

Lesson 5.3Composition All the Way Up

Concept Overview

A professional UI is built in layers of composition, each layer made of the one below. At the bottom are tokens — the raw design values (Module 4). Above them, layout primitives — Stack, Cluster, Grid, and the rest (Module 3) — arrange things. Above those, content components — a Panel, a StatCard, a DataTable — combine primitives and tokens into recognisable, reusable UI pieces, each respecting the ownership boundary and scoped locally (Chapters 5.1–5.2). At the top, a screen — a dashboard, a settings page — is a composition of content components placed by layout primitives. Nothing at any layer is built from scratch; each layer assembles the layer below. A dashboard is not written; it is composed.

Plain English Explanation

Think of how a house is built. You do not start with raw ore — you start with standard bricks. Bricks combine into walls. Walls combine into rooms. Rooms combine into the house. Each level is made from a small set of standard parts from the level below, and nobody re-invents the brick for each house.

A UI is built the same way, in the same kind of levels. The tokens are the raw material — the clay. The layout primitives are the bricks — a small, fixed set of standard arranging pieces. A content component like a Panel is a wall — built by combining primitives (a Stack inside, a header that is a Cluster) and tokens (its padding, its colours), into something recognisable and reusable. A screen like a dashboard is the finished house — built by placing Panels and StatCards into a Grid. The crucial point is that at no level do you start from nothing: every level is assembled from standard, trusted parts from the level below. When someone says a dashboard was "built in a day," this is how — the bricks and walls already existed, and the day was spent arranging them. That is composition, and it is the entire reason professional teams ship interfaces quickly without their quality drifting.

Why It Matters

Composition is what makes a frontend both fast to build and consistent — two things that normally trade off against each other. Because every screen is assembled from the same components, and every component from the same primitives and tokens, a new screen is mostly arrangement, not creation: fast. And because the parts are shared, every screen is automatically consistent and every improvement to a part improves every screen using it: quality. A non-composed codebase has neither — each screen is hand-built, so it is slow to produce and drifts from every other screen. Composition is the single technique that lets a small team build and maintain a large, coherent product. Everything in this course has been building toward enabling it.

Real-World Example

A team needs a new "Reports" screen: a header, four summary stats, and two data panels. In a composed codebase the build is almost entirely assembly. The four stats are four StatCard instances — the component already exists — placed in an intrinsic Grid primitive. The two data panels are two Panel instances, also existing, placed in a Switcher primitive so they sit side by side on desktop and stack on mobile. The engineer writes a thin screen-level file that arranges these existing parts and almost no new CSS. The screen is responsive (the primitives handle it), consistent (the components are shared), and themeable (the tokens flow through), without the engineer solving any of those problems again. The same StatCard and Panel will build the next screen, and the next. That is the daily reality of working in a composed system.

Enterprise Example

An enterprise dashboard product — a trading terminal, a SaaS admin console — is a deep composition tree, and managing that tree is the architecture. The lower layers (tokens, layout primitives, base components) are typically owned by a central design-system team and treated as stable, versioned, well-tested infrastructure. The upper layers (specific dashboard components, whole screens) are owned by product teams who compose them from the shared lower layers. This division — shared, stable foundations; team-owned composition on top — is how a large organisation moves fast without fragmenting: product teams are never blocked re-solving layout or theming, and the design-system team can improve a primitive once and lift every product. The component frameworks the industry uses (React, Svelte, Vue) are, at heart, machinery for exactly this layered composition — each framework component is a node in the tree, composed of the components below it. Module 8 builds six such compositions end to end, and every one is assembled from the layers this course has taught.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

How to build a screen by composition:

  1. Confirm the foundation: design tokens defined (Module 4), and the layout primitives available (Module 3).
  2. Identify the content components the screen needs — Panel, StatCard, DataTable — and for each that does not exist, build it: compose primitives for its internal layout, draw values from tokens, respect the ownership boundary, scope its CSS locally.
  3. Build each content component in isolation first, and verify it works standalone before placing it in a screen.
  4. Build the screen as a thin composition layer: choose layout primitives to place the content components, and let those primitives own the gaps and the responsive behaviour.
  5. Write almost no new CSS at the screen level — a screen is arrangement. New CSS at the screen level usually signals a missing reusable component.
  6. Verify the whole tree: change a token and confirm it flows up; resize and confirm the primitives reflow; reuse a component elsewhere and confirm its boundary holds.

Code Example

css
CSS — a Panel component composed from primitives and tokens/* THE PANEL — a content component. It composes:               */
/*   - a Stack primitive (vertical, gap) for its body           */
/*   - a Cluster-like header (space-between)                    */
/*   - tokens for every value. Owns its inside; no outer margin.*/
.panel {
  background: var(--color-surface);
  border-radius: var(--radius);
  padding: var(--space-4);
  /* the Panel IS a Stack — vertical, token-gapped               */
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  gap: var(--space-4);
}
.panel__head {  /* a Cluster: title left, badge right */
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
}
.panel__body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: var(--space-2); }

/* THE SCREEN — a thin composition: a Grid placing Panels.     */
.dashboard {
  display: grid;
  gap: var(--space-4);
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(260px, 1fr));
}

Responsive Example

In a composed system, responsiveness is not solved at the screen level — it is inherited from the layers below. The screen places Panels in an intrinsic Grid primitive, so the Panels reflow their column count by themselves. Each Panel's internal layout is a Stack, which is responsive on its own. The tokens carry the fluid clamp() scaling. The screen-level file therefore contains no media query at all:

css
CSS — a fully responsive screen with zero screen-level queries/* the SCREEN just composes — responsiveness comes from below: */
.dashboard {
  display: grid;
  gap: var(--space-4);
  /* intrinsic Grid primitive -> recounts columns itself        */
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(min(260px,100%), 1fr));
}
/* no @media here. the Grid reflows, the Stacks reflow,        */
/* the fluid tokens scale — the screen inherits all of it.     */

This is the ultimate payoff of the course's architecture: responsiveness, theming, and consistency are all properties of the lower layers, so a screen composed from those layers gets all three for free. The screen file is short, declarative, and almost entirely arrangement.

Common Mistakes

Common Mistake

Solving layout and theming again at the screen level. If a screen file is full of fresh media queries, raw colours, and bespoke layout, the lower layers are not being used. A screen should be thin — mostly the placement of existing components.

Common Mistake

A "god component" that does everything. One enormous component that is the whole dashboard cannot be reused or tested in parts. Break it into small content components, each composable and independently verifiable.

Common Mistake

Skipping the standalone build. Building a component only ever inside the screen that needs it hides its dependencies on that context. Build and verify each component in isolation first — that is what proves it is genuinely reusable.

Principal Engineer Notes

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer, given a screen to build, first decomposes it into the composition tree — "this is a Grid of Panels; each Panel is a Stack with a Cluster header" — before writing any code. They identify which parts already exist and which must be built, and they build the missing parts as reusable components, not as screen-specific markup. The screen is the last thing assembled, from parts already proven.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

They treat new CSS at the screen level as a signal, not a normal event. When a screen genuinely needs new styling, the principal-level question is "should this be a new reusable component instead?" — because a recurring need belongs in the component layer where every future screen can share it. A composed system gets stronger over time precisely because needs are continually pushed down into reusable layers rather than solved one-off at the top.

Exercises

Practice — Composition
  1. Build a Panel content component: compose a Stack for its body and a space-between Cluster for its header, styled entirely with tokens, with a clean ownership boundary and scoped CSS. Verify it standalone first.
  2. Build a small dashboard screen by placing several Panel and StatCard instances in a Grid primitive. Confirm you wrote almost no screen-level CSS.
  3. Resize the dashboard and confirm it is fully responsive without a single screen-level media query — the responsiveness is inherited from the primitives.
  4. Change one design token and confirm the change flows all the way up through primitives and components to the screen.
Solution & What to Check
  1. The Panel is a Stack (its body) plus a space-between Cluster (its header), styled only with tokens, with no outer margin and scoped CSS. If it works rendered alone, it is genuinely reusable.
  2. A dashboard built by placing Panel and StatCard instances in a Grid needs almost no screen-level CSS — the screen is arrangement, not creation.
  3. The dashboard is responsive with no screen-level media query because the Grid primitive recounts columns and each Panel's Stack reflows on its own — responsiveness is inherited from the layers below.
  4. Changing one token flows up through primitives and components to the screen — proof the composition tree shares a single source of truth.

Architecture Thinking

Layered composition is the master pattern of all engineering, and this chapter is where the course's separate threads reveal themselves as one idea. Tokens bounded a value; primitives bounded an arrangement; components bounded a piece of UI; the global/local split bounded a scope — and at every level the bounded units compose into the level above. That is the whole of it: build small, well-bounded, well-named units, and assemble them into larger units that are themselves small, well-bounded, and well-named. A system built this way can grow without bound, because complexity is partitioned at every level rather than accumulating in any one place, and any piece can be understood, tested, replaced, or improved in isolation. This is why component architecture is the organising idea of modern frontend, and it is the same idea — bounded units, composed — that organises every other well-built software system. If you have followed the course to here, you do not just know CSS techniques; you know the principle the techniques are instances of.

Debugging Tips

  • Debug at the right layer. A bug in a composed UI lives at one layer — a token, a primitive, a component, or the screen. Identify the layer first, then debug there; do not patch a component for what is really a token problem.
  • Test components in isolation. When a component misbehaves inside a screen, pull it out and render it alone. If it works alone, the bug is in the composition; if it breaks alone, the bug is in the component.
  • Read the composition tree in the Elements panel. The DOM tree is the composition tree — screen, primitives, components, elements. Reading it top-down shows you how the UI was assembled and where a layer might be wrong.

Performance Considerations

Performance

Composition is good for performance for the reasons gathered across the course. Reused components mean a small stylesheet — the same rules serve many elements — which downloads and parses fast (Chapter 5.1). Scoped, flat class selectors match quickly (Chapter 5.2). Layout primitives keep responsiveness in the engine and out of JavaScript resize logic (Module 3). Tokens keep values de-duplicated (Module 4). The one performance discipline composition adds is to watch nesting depth: a composition tree can become very deep, and every layout recalculation walks it, so keep the tree as shallow as the design honestly allows. And for components rendering large data — a table of thousands of rows — composition does not remove the need to virtualise. Module 7, next, is the full treatment of performance: the rendering pipeline, layout cost, animation, and the maintainability that a composed architecture makes possible.

Build This — Mini-Project
A Dashboard Composed from a Reusable Panel

The capstone of Module 5: build one reusable Panel component — composed from a Stack and a space-between header, styled with tokens, with a clean boundary and scoped CSS — then compose a dashboard by placing two Panel instances in a Grid. You will see the same component reused, and a responsive screen built with almost no screen-level CSS.

Goal: a responsive dashboard assembled from a reusable Panel component placed by a layout primitive.
html
index.html<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Reusable Primitives</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
</head>
<body>
  <!-- THE SCREEN: a Grid placing two Panel components -->
  <div class="dashboard">
    <section class="panel">
      <header class="panel__head">
        <h3 class="panel__title">Transactions</h3>
        <span class="panel__badge">12</span>
      </header>
      <div class="panel__body">
        <p>Panel body content one.</p>
        <p>Panel body content two.</p>
      </div>
    </section>
    <!-- the SAME component, reused -->
    <section class="panel">
      <header class="panel__head">
        <h3 class="panel__title">Alerts</h3>
        <span class="panel__badge">3</span>
      </header>
      <div class="panel__body">
        <p>Another instance of the same reusable Panel.</p>
      </div>
    </section>
  </div>
</body>
</html>
css
styles.css/* TOKENS — the foundation layer (Module 4)                    */
:root { --space: 16px; --surface: #fff; --brand: #2b59ff; }
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; }
body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; background: #eef1f5; padding: var(--space); }

/* THE SCREEN — a thin Grid composition. Owns the gaps only.   */
.dashboard {
  display: grid; gap: var(--space);
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(min(260px,100%), 1fr));
}

/* THE PANEL — a reusable component: a Stack + a Cluster head, */
/* token-styled, clean boundary (no outer margin), scoped CSS. */
.panel {
  background: var(--surface); border-radius: 10px; padding: var(--space);
  display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: var(--space);
}
.panel__head {  /* Cluster: title left, badge right */
  display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;
}
.panel__title { color: #16267a; font-size: 15px; }
.panel__badge {
  background: var(--brand); color: #fff;
  font-size: 12px; font-weight: 700; padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 999px;
}
.panel__body {  /* Stack: vertical, gapped */
  display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 6px;
  color: #404654; font-size: 14px;
}

Now see composition at work:

  1. Two panels render — identical structure, different content. One component definition, two instances. The screen-level CSS is just the .dashboard Grid: four lines.
  2. Resize the window. The dashboard reflows its columns and the panels stack on narrow screens — with no screen-level media query. The responsiveness is inherited from the Grid primitive.
  3. Change the --space token to 28px. The dashboard gap, panel padding, and panel internal gaps all grow together — one token, flowing up through every layer.
  4. In DevTools' Elements panel, read the tree top-down: dashboard → panel → head/body → elements. That tree is the composition tree of Figure 5.3.
Expected result: a responsive two-panel dashboard, assembled from one reusable Panel component placed by a Grid primitive and styled with tokens. The screen file is tiny; responsiveness, consistency and theming are all inherited from the layers below — composition, working exactly as designed.
06
Module Six

Advanced Responsive Engineering

A marketing page has known content and a few layouts. A dashboard has unpredictable real data, deeply nested layouts, and many states. This module is about engineering for that harder reality: why dashboards break, how defensive CSS prevents it, and how to debug a broken enterprise layout fast.

6.1Why Dashboards Are Harder Than Websites
6.2Defensive CSS & Content Unpredictability
6.3Debugging Broken Enterprise Layouts
Module 6 · Chapter 6.1

Why Dashboards Are Harder Than Websites

A marketing website and an enterprise dashboard are both "responsive CSS," but they are not the same difficulty of problem. Understanding exactly why the dashboard is harder is what lets you engineer for it deliberately instead of being surprised by it.

Lesson 6.1The Dashboard Difficulty

Concept Overview

A dashboard is harder than a website along four specific axes. Content is unknown: a website's text is written by the team and fixed; a dashboard renders live data — names, numbers, table rows — whose length and quantity the team cannot predict. Layouts are deeply nested: a website is mostly a single column of sections; a dashboard is layouts inside layouts inside layouts, and Module 3 showed how nesting propagates failures. Space is contested: a website scrolls freely, but a dashboard often must fit many panels into one non-scrolling viewport at once. States are many: a website element is usually just "there," while a dashboard panel has loading, empty, error, partial, and overflowing states, each of which must hold the layout. A website is a known layout with known content; a dashboard is a layout system that must survive unknown content in many states. That difference is the whole module.

Plain English Explanation

Think of the difference between framing a single photograph and designing a display case for a museum. Framing one photo is straightforward: you have the photo in your hand, you know its exact size, you cut the frame to fit. That is a marketing website — the content is known and fixed, and you build the layout around it.

A dashboard is the museum display case. You are building it before you know what will go in it, it must hold many objects at once, those objects arrive in unpredictable sizes, some slots will sometimes be empty and some objects will be too big, and the whole case has a fixed footprint it must not exceed. You cannot "cut it to fit," because there is nothing fixed to fit to. You have to engineer a case that holds whatever shows up — short names and absurdly long ones, three rows and three thousand, a panel mid-load and a panel that failed. That is why dashboard layout is engineering rather than decoration: you are not arranging known content, you are building a system robust to content you will never see in advance. The rest of this module is the techniques for building that robust case.

Why It Matters

Most dashboard layout bugs ship because the team unconsciously treated the dashboard like a website — they built it around the seed data on their own machine, which is short and tidy and complete. Then production data arrives: the long company name, the empty result set, the table with eighty columns, the slow-loading panel — and the layout that looked perfect in development falls apart. Naming the four axes of difficulty turns this from a recurring surprise into a checklist. Before shipping a dashboard, an engineer who has internalised this chapter asks: have I tested unknown content, deep nesting, contested space, and every state? That question prevents most of the bugs the rest of the module teaches you to fix.

Real-World Example

A team builds a "Recent Activity" dashboard panel. Their development database has five activity rows with short descriptions, and the panel looks great. They ship. In production, three things happen on day one. A power user has two hundred activity rows, and the panel — with no fixed height or scroll — stretches down the entire page. A new account has zero rows, and the panel collapses to a thin empty sliver because nothing gave it a minimum height. And one activity description is a pasted error message four hundred characters long, which has no spaces, so it does not wrap and instead pushes the panel — and the whole dashboard — wider than the screen. None of these appeared in development because development data was tidy. All three are the "content is unknown" axis. The panel was built like a website element; it needed to be engineered like a dashboard component.

Enterprise Example

Enterprises turn the four axes into process. Because content is unknown, they test with deliberately hostile fixture data — the longest plausible string, the empty set, the maximum row count — as a standard part of building any data-driven component; some teams keep a shared "torture-test" dataset for exactly this. Because layouts are deeply nested, they enforce the min-width: 0 discipline of Module 3.6 and keep nesting shallow. Because space is contested, dashboard panels are given explicit sizing strategies — fixed-height-with-internal-scroll, or a defined share of a grid — rather than being allowed to grow freely. Because states are many, every data component is required to define and style its loading, empty, and error states as first-class deliverables, not afterthoughts. The enterprise mindset is that a dashboard component is not "done" when it renders the happy path with seed data — it is done when it has been proven against unknown content in every state. Module 8's dashboard projects are built and tested this way.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

How to approach a dashboard layout deliberately:

  1. Assume content is unknown. For every piece of dynamic content, ask: what if it is very long, very short, very plentiful, or absent?
  2. Map the nesting. Identify how many layout levels deep the component sits, and apply the min-width: 0 chain (Module 3.6) through it.
  3. Decide each panel's space strategy. Will it grow freely, take a fixed grid share, or have a fixed height with internal scroll? Decide explicitly — do not let it default.
  4. Enumerate the states. List loading, empty, error, partial, and overflowing for each data component, and design the layout to hold all of them.
  5. Test with hostile data. Replace tidy seed data with the longest string, the empty set, and the maximum count, and confirm the layout survives.
  6. Only then consider it done. A dashboard that works with seed data is not finished; one proven against unknown content in every state is.

Code Example

css
CSS — a dashboard panel engineered for the four axes/* SPACE: a fixed-height panel that scrolls its own body —     */
/* so 3 rows or 3000 rows, the panel keeps its footprint.      */
.activity-panel {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  height: 320px;      /* contested space: a fixed footprint */
  min-width: 0;        /* nesting: stays shrinkable (Module 3.6) */
}
.activity-panel__body {
  flex: 1;
  overflow-y: auto;  /* unknown row count: scroll, do not stretch */
}

/* STATE: an empty state that still fills the panel's space.   */
.activity-panel__empty {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  flex: 1;          /* zero rows still does not collapse the panel */
}

Responsive Example

Dashboard responsiveness is harder than website responsiveness because it is responsive in two dimensions at once: the viewport changes and the content changes. A panel must hold its layout when the window narrows and when the data inside it is unexpectedly long or plentiful. The techniques are the ones the course has built — intrinsic grids, the min-width: 0 chain, fixed-footprint panels with internal scroll — applied together:

css
CSS — a panel robust to viewport AND content change.dashboard {
  display: grid; gap: 16px;
  /* viewport axis: intrinsic grid recounts columns            */
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(min(280px,100%), 1fr));
}
.dashboard > * {
  min-width: 0;      /* content axis: panels stay shrinkable */
  overflow: hidden;  /* content axis: wide content cannot break out */
}

A dashboard "is responsive" only when it holds across both axes — a layout that survives a narrow window but breaks on a long company name is not responsive in the sense a dashboard requires.

Common Mistakes

Common Mistake

Building and testing with tidy seed data. Development data is short, complete, and plentiful-but-not-too-plentiful — nothing like production. A dashboard tested only against seed data is untested against the content that will actually break it.

Common Mistake

Ignoring the empty and error states. Teams design the happy path and forget that a panel will sometimes have no data or failed to load. An undesigned empty state usually collapses the layout; an undesigned error state usually leaves a blank hole.

Common Mistake

Letting panels grow freely. A panel with no height strategy stretches to fit its content, and on a dashboard that means one data-heavy panel pushes everything else off the screen. Panels need an explicit space strategy.

Principal Engineer Notes

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer builds dashboard components against hostile data from the first moment, not the happy path. Before they trust a panel, they have already put the longest plausible string, the empty set, and ten thousand rows through it. They treat tidy seed data as a trap — the thing that makes a broken layout look finished — and they distrust any component that has only been seen with it.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

They treat every state of a data component as a deliverable with equal weight. "Loading," "empty," and "error" are not edge cases to bolt on later — they are part of the component's definition, designed and built alongside the happy path.
