Styling for the full range of possible content — truncation, wrapping, reserved space — not just the tidy example (Ch 6.2).
Layout thrashing
Interleaving DOM reads and writes in a loop, forcing the browser to re-run layout synchronously each iteration (Ch 7.1).
contain / content-visibility
Properties that isolate a region's layout so its updates do not affect the rest of the page (Ch 7.1).
Compositor
The engine stage — often GPU, off the main thread — that moves and fades pre-painted layers; the fast path for animation (Ch 7.2).
Virtualisation
Rendering only the rows visible in a scroll viewport and recycling them, so a huge dataset keeps a small DOM (Ch 6.1, 7.1).
Technical debt
The accumulated cost of past shortcuts that make every future change harder (Ch 7.3).
Refactoring
Improving code structure without changing what it does or renders (Ch 7.3).
:has()
The relational selector — targets an element based on what it contains. Replaces JavaScript class toggling for many state patterns (Ch 1.3).
:is() / :where()
Selector group functions; :is() takes the highest-specificity of its arguments, :where() contributes zero specificity (Ch 1.3).
light-dark()
A CSS function returning the first or second value depending on the resolved color-scheme — one declaration handles both themes (Ch 4.3).
color-mix()
A CSS function that mixes two colours at a percentage in a named colour space; the modern way to derive hover and disabled states from a base token (Ch 4.3).
oklch()
A perceptually-uniform colour space — lightness, chroma, hue — that gives consistent visual brightness across hues (Ch 4.3).
Native CSS nesting
Authoring nested rules directly in CSS using & for the parent selector — no preprocessor required (Ch 5.2).
subgrid
A grid template value that inherits the parent grid's track lines, so nested grids can align to the outer grid (Back Matter — Modern CSS).
@property
Registers a typed, animatable custom property with an initial value and inheritance behaviour (Back Matter — Modern CSS).
@starting-style
Defines the starting value of an animated property when an element first appears, enabling pure-CSS entry animations (Back Matter).
interpolate-size
Opts into transitioning to and from intrinsic keyword sizes like auto — the fix for "animate to height: auto" (Back Matter).
View Transitions
Browser-managed animations between two DOM states; the engine snapshots before-and-after and animates between them (Back Matter).
Popover API
The native popover HTML attribute that gives any element top-layer rendering, a backdrop, and light-dismiss behaviour without a library (Back Matter).
Anchor positioning
The anchor-name / position-anchor / anchor() system that pins one element to another's edges without portals or JavaScript (Back Matter).
field-sizing
A property that makes <input> and <textarea> grow to fit their content (Back Matter).
Where to go next

You have completed a full course in CSS architecture. The natural next layers of a frontend engineer's craft are a component framework (React, Svelte, or Vue), TypeScript, automated testing, and accessibility in depth — each of which composes with everything you have learned here. The mental models you built — systems over pages, design for the input range, optimise for the next change, compose bounded parts — are not CSS-specific. They are how good software is built everywhere. Carry them forward.

Back Matter

Modern CSS — The 2026 Frontier

CSS has changed substantially in the last three years. This chapter is a curated catalogue of the features that have reached baseline support since 2023 and that a 2026-current frontend engineer is expected to know. Earlier chapters wove the most central of these into their lessons; this one gathers the rest, each with the architectural problem it solves.

Layout — subgrid

The long-missing piece of CSS Grid. When a child of a grid item is itself a grid, by default its tracks are independent of its parent's — so two sibling cards cannot align their internal columns to each other. subgrid fixes this: a nested grid can declare grid-template-columns: subgrid (or grid-template-rows: subgrid) and inherit its parent's track lines, so its content aligns to the outer grid. This is the right tool whenever you have a row of cards whose interior elements — titles, prices, buttons — must line up across cards.

css
CSS — cards whose interiors align via subgrid.card-row { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); grid-template-rows: auto auto auto; }
.card {
  display: grid;
  grid-row: span 3;
  grid-template-rows: subgrid;   /* inherit parent's rows */
}

Tokens — @property

Plain CSS custom properties are untyped strings — which means the browser cannot animate them (it does not know whether --brand holds a colour, a number, or a length), and an invalid value silently breaks the rule. @property registers a custom property with a type, an initial value, and inheritance behaviour. Once registered, the property is type-checked, has a sensible default if missing, and — critically — can be animated. This unlocks one of the long-standing gaps: smooth transitions on token values.

css
CSS — a typed, animatable custom property@property --brand-hue {
  syntax: '<number>';
  inherits: true;
  initial-value: 265;
}
.btn {
  background: oklch(55% 0.22 var(--brand-hue));
  transition: --brand-hue .3s;
}
.btn:hover { --brand-hue: 320; }  /* smoothly shifts hue */

Animation — @starting-style, interpolate-size, View Transitions

Three pieces close some of the most stubborn animation gaps in CSS.

@starting-style defines the value an element should start at on first render, so a transition can run as the element appears. Before this, entry animations needed a JavaScript class toggle on next-tick — now they are pure CSS.

interpolate-size: allow-keywords finally allows transitioning to and from intrinsic keyword sizes — height: 0 → height: auto works, the long-standing pain point of expanding/collapsing panels.

The View Transitions API animates the change between two states of the DOM — when content swaps, View Transitions cross-fades or morphs between the before-and-after frames automatically, with the browser handling the snapshotting. Same-document transitions are baseline; cross-document transitions are the newest tier and have shipped in Chromium-based browsers.

css
CSS — entry animation and height:auto transition/* entry animation: starts at opacity 0, transitions to 1       */
.toast {
  opacity: 1;
  transition: opacity .2s;
}
@starting-style {
  .toast { opacity: 0; }
}

/* expand-to-auto: opt the page in, then animate height: auto. */
:root { interpolate-size: allow-keywords; }
.panel { height: 0; transition: height .3s; overflow: hidden; }
.panel.is-open { height: auto; }

Overlays — Popover API and Anchor Positioning

Two related platform features finally make tooltips, menus, and modals declarative rather than hand-built. The Popover API — the popover HTML attribute, opened by a popovertarget button — gives any element first-class popover behaviour: top-layer rendering, a backdrop, light-dismiss on click outside, ESC to close, focus management. None of that requires a library.

Anchor positioning — anchor-name on the anchor element, position-anchor and the anchor()/anchor-size() functions on the positioned element — pins one element to another's edges, without portals or JavaScript measurement. The classic "tooltip below this button, flipping above when there's no room" pattern becomes declarative CSS.

html
HTML + CSS — a popover anchored to its trigger<button id="open" popovertarget="menu">Menu</button>
<div id="menu" popover>...</div>

#open { anchor-name: --open; }
#menu {
  position-anchor: --open;
  top: anchor(bottom);
  left: anchor(left);
}

Inputs — field-sizing

A small but long-requested fix. field-sizing: content on an <input> or <textarea> makes the field grow to fit its content, rather than holding a fixed size. The "auto-growing textarea" used to require JavaScript measuring the text and writing a height; now it is one CSS line.

Where these fit in the course's architecture

None of these features replace what the course taught — they extend it. The composition tree (Module 5) is the same; subgrid and anchor positioning are new tools within it. The token layer (Module 4) is the same; @property and color-mix() make tokens richer and animatable. The performance discipline (Module 7) is the same; View Transitions and @starting-style move more animation work onto the engine's fast path, away from JavaScript. The shape of an enterprise CSS architecture has not changed in 2026 — the toolkit inside it has, and a current engineer should reach for the new tools when they are the right answer.

How Principal Engineers (L7+) Think

A principal engineer adopts new CSS features by the same rule they apply to any new tool: does this remove code I currently have to write or maintain? :has() removes a JavaScript class toggle. light-dark() removes a duplicated theme block. color-mix() removes a stack of hand-picked state tokens. View Transitions removes a custom animation library. Anchor positioning removes a portal and a resize listener. Each is a small, focused subtraction — and a codebase that adopts them shrinks, which is exactly what Module 7's maintainability discipline asks for. Modern CSS earns its place by deleting code, not adding API surface.
